---
Title: Cover Letter and Responses to Editor and Reviewer Concerns
format:
  hikmah-response-typst: default
bibliography: bibliography.bib
---

Dear editors of Behavioral Research Methods,

Thank you for the opportunity to revise and resubmit our manuscript, ‚ÄúA Beta Way: A Bayesian Tutorial on Beta Regression for Psychological Research" (Manuscript IDBR-TSC-25-031). We found the editor‚Äôs and reviewers‚Äô comments to be insightful and constructive, and we have used them to substantially revise and strengthen the manuscript. In this memo, we describe how we addressed each point. Reviewers‚Äô comments are cited or summarized in red italicized text, and our responses appear in black Roman text. Excerpts from the revised manuscript are shown in blue. All added or modified text in the manuscript is indicated in bold. We have included both a marked-up and a clean version of the revised manuscript. Before addressing the specific comments in turn, we would like to highlight several important changes made to the manuscript.

First, we have revised the statistical language throughout to better reflect a Bayesian framework. We recognized that some of our original phrasing ‚Äî particularly around credible intervals ‚Äî could be interpreted as frequentist in nature. We have therefore shifted the focus toward the probability of direction (pd) (a subjective probability measure of the degree of certainty in the direction of the parameter) and use credible intervals as a measure of uncertainty. We hope these revisions satisfy the concerns raised by the editor and reviewers. Second, we have added a section on Bayes factors and how to compute them in brms. We note, however, that because we use default priors, Bayes factors for point-null hypotheses are not available in our primary analyses; we address this limitation directly in the manuscript.

Third, we have updated the title of the paper to more explicitly reflect its Bayesian focus so as not to confuse readers.

Fourth, although we disagree with Reviewer 1's characterization of the dataset as poor, we agree that an additional example could strengthen the paper. We therefore selected a second example from the same published paper ‚Äî one that includes judgments of learning (JOLs) with true boundary responses (0s and 1s) ‚Äî and fit an ordered beta regression model to these data.

Finally, in response to reviewer feedback, we have added an appendix demonstrating how to conduct beta regression using frequentist packages, including {glmmTMB}. We chose to present this material in an appendix rather than integrating it into the main text, as doing so would disrupt the narrative flow of the tutorial. We hope this approach both addresses the reviewers' concerns and keeps the manuscript accessible to readers with varying statistical backgrounds.

Regards,

Jason Geller

Robert Kubinec

Matti Vuore

Chelsea Parlett-Pelleriti

# Reviewer 1

## Major Comments

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: The paper restricts beta regression examples to the brms package, without mentioning alternatives, even in R such as betareg. Although the authors have chosen to do this because they want to stay within a Bayesian framework, it would be helpful for readers if they at least mention that betareg is available for those working in a frequentist framework. They also should provide at least one example using both betareg and brms, preferably the first example in their paper.
:::

We thank the reviewer for this suggestion. In an earlier draft we did include side-by-side comparisons of brms and betareg/glmmTMB output. However, we found that doing so diluted the pedagogical focus of the tutorial ‚Äî an issue the reviewer themselves articulates clearly later in this review when describing the "classic pedagogical error" of combining new statistical concepts with new software environments in a single lesson. We agree with this concern, and on reflection it applies equally to weaving Bayesian and frequentist implementations together in the main exposition. To address the reviewer's concern while preserving pedagogical clarity, we have made two changes. First, we have revised the title to make the Bayesian focus explicit from the outset, so readers can make an informed decision about the tutorial's scope before investing time in it. Second, we have added a frequentist appendix demonstrating how to fit the equivalent models using {glmmTMB}, which supports the full range of beta-family models discussed in the tutorial (including zero-inflated) and extends naturally to mixed-effects specifications ‚Äî a scope betareg alone does not cover. We hope these changes address the reviewer's concern.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: The authors seem aware of this problem because in that section they set about trying to teach some basics about Bayesian estimation. So now they're trying to teach (and sell) two things simultaneously rather than one, which is a classic pedagogical error (e.g., those of us who teach stats know better than to combine new statistical concepts and new software environments in the same lesson). If they are going to tread this path, then the authors should back up claims such as the similarity between estimates using noninformative priors and estimates using max-likelihood with an example comparing outputs from brms and betareg. If the authors want to maximize the utility of their tutorial, they need to include examples using frequentist methods and provide meaningful comparisons with their Bayesian counterparts. For example, here is the output from betareg for their "fluency" example, using the data-set with the zeros and ones removed (as in their example). It is clear that the coefficients in both the mean and precision submodels are close to their counterparts in the Beta Regression column of Table 8. Straightforward demonstrations like this could be provided for the ZIB and ZOIB models as well, using gamlss.
:::

We have addressed this by including frequentist examples in Appendix A.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: A second major qualm I have with the paper is its reliance on the one small data-set for all of its models and demonstrations. The fluency data is not a high-quality data-set, and on its own it does not present a convincing case that beta regression can be applied to psychologically relevant research questions. It certainly doesn't do as well on this count than earlier tutorials such as the Smithson-Verkuilen (2006) paper, whose reading-ability example convincingly demonstrated why modelling heteroscedasticity could reveal important effects in the mean submodel. This paper either needs a better example or more than one example. There are many, many psychologically relevant data-sets out there whose dependent variables are doubly-bounded; surely the authors can come up with a better example than the fluency data.
:::

We thank the reviewer for this suggestion and have added a second case study to address it. The new example uses judgments of learning (JOLs) from the same Wilford et al. (2020) study and demonstrates the application of ordered beta regression‚Äîa model class that is not as naturally motivated by the fluency‚Äìaccuracy example alone. Using two outcomes from the same experimental context is, in our view, a pedagogical strength rather than a limitation. It allows readers to see how different bounded dependent variables within a single, familiar paradigm can require different beta-family specifications, without the added cognitive burden of learning a new experimental context alongside a new modeling framework.

Regarding the fluency‚Äìaccuracy data itself, we respectfully maintain that it is well suited for the tutorial‚Äôs goals. The dataset is published and peer-reviewed, exhibits the doubly bounded structure that beta regression is designed to handle, and‚Äîcritically‚Äîincludes boundary values (zeros and a one) that motivate the progression through increasingly flexible beta-family models. A ‚Äúcleaner‚Äù dataset without such boundary values would, in fact, be less informative for demonstrating these modeling decisions.

We agree that Smithson and Verkuilen‚Äôs (2006) reading-ability example is exemplary in illustrating heteroscedasticity in the mean submodel. Importantly, our fluency dataset reveals a related but distinct issue: failing to appropriately model the data-generating process can lead to misleading inferences. Under more traditional analyses, fluency appears to be a significant predictor of recall. However, when we model the data using beta regression and its extensions that account for boundary values, this conclusion becomes less clear. Specifically, the apparent effect of fluency is not primarily on the conditional mean of the response, but instead reflects differences in the probability of boundary outcomes (e.g., zero recall). This distinction highlights the importance of selecting models that align with the underlying structure of the data. 


::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: The poor quality of the fluency data is underscored by the authors' misspecification of the ZOIB model. Given that there is only one occurrence of a 1 in the Accuracy variable, it is ludicrous to predict it with Fluency as the authors do (moreover, it occurs in the Disfluent condition!). That part of the ZOIB model should be eliminated‚ÄîNo-one who is competent with logistic regression would include it.
:::

The reviewer is correct, and we thank them for catching this. With only a single observation at 1 in the Accuracy variable, modeling the one-inflation component as a function of Fluency is not statistically defensible ‚Äî the conditional probability estimate for that submodel is effectively undefined, and we should not have presented it as a substantive comparison. We have revised the ZOIB specification in the fluency example so that the one-inflation submodel is intercept-only, treating the probability of a 1 as a constant rather than a function of the predictor. The zero-inflation submodel continues to be modeled as a function of Fluency, since the zeros are well-represented in both conditions. We also use this revision as an opportunity to discuss with readers when it is and is not appropriate to model the inflation components as functions of predictors ‚Äî a point we had not made explicit in the original draft. The additional JOL case study, which contains a more substantial number of boundary observations, provides a complementary demonstration of ZOIB and ordered beta models in a setting where the boundary processes can be meaningfully modeled.

At the same time, we note that this issue only applies to the ZOIB/ZIB models, as ordered beta regression can be estimated with or without observations at the boundaries (i.e. 0 or 1). Without such observations, ordered beta regression naturally collapses to the standard beta regression model.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: My third qualm is the authors' treatment of the boundary-cases problem. They advise readers not to shift zeros and ones away from the boundary, but then they present a much worse practice in their beta regression example‚Äîjust leave 'em out altogether! The paper needs a much more considered discussion of the boundary values problem, including advice to readers about when zeros and ones should be regarded as true versus censored scores, exact versus approximate scores, and when they should be regarded as generated by a separate process from that which produces values in (0,1). Otherwise, readers are left with no criteria for choosing among alternatives such as ZOIB, ordered beta, or extended beta regression. No tutorial on beta regression can be complete without this discussion.
:::

Both options are problematic and we advise readers not to use either approach in the paper, but to fit the traditional beta model we cannot have 0s and 1s. We thank Reviewer 1 for raising this important point. We agree that our original discussion of boundary values (0s and 1s) was insufficient and could leave readers without clear guidance. We have revised the manuscript to include a more detailed discussion of how boundary values can arise, and how modeling choices should depend on the underlying data-generating process. Specifically, we now distinguish between cases where boundary values reflect true structural outcomes versus those where they arise from measurement or approximation, and we outline modeling approaches appropriate to each scenario (e.g., zero/one-inflated beta models, ordered beta models, or transformations). While no single rule can determine the appropriate approach in all cases, we provide practical guidelines to help readers make informed decisions based on theory and measurement considerations.

To clarify our discussion of excluding/including boundary values, we presented the material in this method because while this is not an optimal practice for social science research, it is statistically valid. A beta regression is appropriately defined over all strictly continuous responses; the remaining discrete responses can be separately modeled with the Bernoulli/logistic regression. Both models are appropriately identified in their domain without bias. The intention in presenting the data in this method was not to suggest that scholars *should* do this in their papers (i.e. use separate sub-models), but rather to provide a helpful foundation before presenting methods that combine these two distributions, i.e., the ZOIB/ZIB/ordered beta, as we have seen that scholars find these models puzzling conceptually.

## Minor Points

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: Line 135: The claim that the beta distribution is the distribution for the probability of an event is inaccurate. The two most common derivations of the beta distribution are from a proportion of one chi-square variable out of the sum of it with another independent chi-square variable, V2=X12/(X12+X22)V\^2 = X_1\^2 / (X_1\^2 + X_2\^2) V2=X12‚Äã/(X12‚Äã+X22‚Äã), or a proportion of one gamma variable out of the sum of it with another independent gamma variable that shares one of its parameters with the first variable. I'm not recommending that the authors include this in their paper, but they should omit their claim about the beta distribution being a distribution of probabilities. Given that their main point is that the beta distribution captures everything in (0,1) nothing is lost thereby.
:::

We thank Reviewer 1 for this helpful clarification and have revised the language in the beta distribution section accordingly. While we do not have time to delve fully into its derivation, the beta distribution is useful for modeling bounded continuous scales because it is defined on the open interval (0, 1). This makes it well-suited for representing proportions and rates, which are naturally constrained to this range. By varying its two shape parameters, the beta distribution can take on a wide variety of forms (e.g., symmetric, skewed, U-shaped, uniform), allowing it to flexibly capture different patterns of variability commonly observed in proportional data. As a result, when a response variable is continuous and bounded between 0 and 1, the beta distribution often provides a convenient and interpretable model for its variability, although its suitability should be evaluated in light of the specific characteristics of the data.

There are two reasons why the beta distribution can be referred as a "distribution for probabilities". The first is that the beta distribution is usually motivated as the conjugate prior distribution for the Bernoulli distribution. That is, the beta distribution can be used to represent a prior belief on the parameter $p$ of the Bernoulli distribution, which represents the probability of success in binary trial. The posterior distribution of Bernoulli with a beta prior on $p$ yields the Beta-Binomial distribution, which is widely used in applied statistics. As the reviewer notes, there are many fascinating connections between distributions in the exponential family, and many other ways that the beta distribution could be motivated, but we note that this is the canonical representation of the beta distribution in statistics.

The second reason has to do with the principle of maximum entropy. The beta distribution, as part of the exponential family, is the maximum entropy distribution for the domain $(0,1)$ subject to the constraint that the $log$ of the expected value of the distribution is a known constant $(E[\text{log }Y]=c)$. Why is this relevant? The logarithm of the expected value is equivalent to the *geometric* mean, which is commonly used to represent the mean of percentiles/proportions. That is, if one is averaging percentile changes, then the geometric mean is more appropriate than a simple average (and is widely used in finance and accounting for those purposes). A percentile is functionally equivalent to a probability and the two are often referred to similarly such as when discussing the change in probability of an outcome (i.e., the outcome became 1.2 percent more likely). As such, it is natural to think of the beta distribution as a distribution that uniquely captures elements of what we think probabilities are.

In other words, if someone begins with the condition that they want to model a *geometric* mean (average of percentile changes), then the beta distribution naturally follows.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: Line 136: "Given that a probability can take on any value from near 0‚Ä¶" is inaccurate. Probabilities can equal 0.
:::

We have changed this paragraph and omitted this sentence.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: Line 156: When both shape parameters equal 1 then the beta distribution is exactly uniform, not "approximately uniform".
:::

We have changed this to just uniform.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: Line 170-171: "while the dispersion, which is inversely related to variance, ‚Ä¶" should be "while the precision, which is inversely related to variance, ‚Ä¶".
:::

We made this change.

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 1: Line 599: "then the beta" should be "than the beta".
:::

We made this change.

::: memo-reviewer 
Reviewer 1: While the terms "zero-inflated model" or "zero-and-one inflated model" continue to be used for beta regression hurdle models, "hurdle model" is the correct term because beta regression models don't predict either zeros or ones (e.g., "zero-inflated Poisson model" is an accurate description because a Poisson model does predict zeros). It would be good practice for a tutorial of this kind to inform readers about this and to use these terms correctly.
:::

We thank the reviewer for this point and understand the underlying distinction: "inflation" in its strict sense refers to excess mass at values the base distribution can itself produce (as with zero-inflated Poisson), whereas the beta distribution assigns zero density to the boundary points, making "hurdle" a more technically precise description. However, we have chosen to retain "zero-inflated beta" (ZIB) and "zero-one-inflated beta" (ZOIB) terminology in the main text for two reasons. First, these are the dominant terms in the applied beta regression literature and in the software readers will interact with ‚Äî brms, glmmTMB, and betareg all use "zero_inflated_beta" family names, and readers searching documentation or forums will find these terms, not "hurdle beta." Using non-matching terminology in a tutorial that aims to get readers running models would introduce friction at exactly the wrong moment. Second, the terminology debate is largely settled in practice even if it remains contested in principle. To acknowledge the distinction without disrupting readers' ability to connect the tutorial to the software, we have added a footnote at the first introduction of ZIB noting that some authors prefer "hurdle model" for these specifications on the grounds the reviewer describes, with a citation for readers who want to pursue the terminological point further. Footnote 2: ‚ÄúSome authors prefer the term hurdle model on the grounds that the beta distribution assigns zero density to the boundary points 0 and 1, so boundary observations are generated by a separate process rather than "inflating" mass that the base distribution could produce. We use zero-inflated beta (ZIB) and zero-one-inflated beta (ZOIB) because these are the standard terms in the applied beta regression literature and match the family names used in modeling packages.‚Äù

# Reviewer 2

## Paper Comments/Edits

This reviewer made numerous comments in the paper (far too many to list here). We have made these changes in the manuscript in bold.

## Git repo

::: memo-reviewer
Reviewer 2: The links to Nix in the README are broken.
:::

These have now been fixed. 

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: The name of the folder in the README is wrong: it should be beta_regression_tutorial, but note that the .Rproj file is named nix-beta_reg_ms.Rproj (these could be made consistent). 
:::

We have updated the .Rproj file and Positron workspace to use a consistent name. 

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: quarto render "\~/manuscript/ms.qmd" generates an error and should be quarto render manuscript/ms.qmd instead. The authors might also want to set the Quarto option exec-dir to "project" so that relative paths are resolved relative to the project root directory. 
::: 

We have made both of these corrections. 

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: There were issues setting up the Nix environment at first, but as documented in issue #55 (Nix build failed due to duplicate RPATH), this was resolved. 
::: 

We have addressed this issue and thank this reviewer for actually testing the Nix/rix setup. We have also added additional documentation to the README clarifying that cmdstan must be installed outside the Nix environment. 

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: When setting up the project without Nix, some packages required for rendering were missing beyond those listed in the README ‚Äî including magick, which appears to be required by the APA extension to crop figures when rendering to PDF.
:::

We were unable to reproduce this issue on our end. The nix environment runs successfully to reproduce the paper. Based on this, we list all the packages needed to reproduce the analyses and figures in the paper.

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: I suggest committing the model output files to Git. Requiring users to correctly configure the environment and run the models from scratch introduces overhead that may be a barrier. 
:::

 We considered this option but prefer that readers generate the models themselves, as doing so is central to the tutorial's pedagogical goals. That said, we do maintain a separate branch that includes model output, which is used to render the paper's accompanying website. 

::: {.memo-reviewer} Reviewer 2: When cloning the repository, the download was over 6 GB despite the working directory being very small. This suggests large files were previously committed and later removed. I recommend auditing the repository history for large files and cleaning up the commit history. 
::: 

We have audited and cleaned the Git history; the repository is now significantly smaller.
